moment_text: Um, and I was in the bathroom cleaning the bathroom and I must have turned my head really fast, you know, and the side of my head got this ceramic towel hanger. So it hit my head so hard that I suddenly felt like, oh, that didn't feel good. And at the same time, my heart just dropped, you know, as if it kind of knew that something had shifted. Um, so I kind of walked around a little bit in a daze the whole day, but I said, oh, I'm just gonna go for a walk. Maybe that's going to sort of. Eliminate this feeling of like doom and gloom. Um, I came back, I cooked and, but I had a really sort of restless night and so I thought I'll just go see my doctor. So I went and saw her. She said, outwardly doesn't look like anything is bad, but I would suggest you go see a dentist. And I was like, why is she asking me to go see a dentist? Like I hit my head. Um. And I didn't, you know, and I think that was sort of the biggest mistake I made, which is not listening to the doctor because obviously she felt like if something has hit your head, it's kind of affected your dental region as well. Um, and so by the time I went to the dentist, um, the infection had set in. My whole body was kind of in the state of absolute panic. And when I went to the dentist, she said like, oh, you're. Tooth had sort of cracked in half and you know, that infection spread to my whole body. And you know, there were days where I felt like I'm just gonna die. You know, I sat at my, you know, my, um, sort of. Sofa in my living room and with like an orange, um, you know, glass of orange juice. And I kept saying to myself, I dunno what's happening. Like, it was the scariest thing, you know? So that was one thing which completely shifted the access for my mental health at that time.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong emotional signals (fear, overwhelm) and barrier signals (delayed diagnosis) related to a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: And then in the wake of that came a few other things. You know, I lost my job. I wasn't tenured, you know, the year after I lost my father and the year after that, um. You know, president Trump came in. So that also affected my mental health in a way. Um, but I think for me it has always been about how do I keep sort of. Sitting and standing upright, even in the face of such physical challenges. Right. Um, for me it has been about, um, asking for help. Right? I didn't grow up, you know, with that kind of messaging. You know, my system was not trained to sort of say. I'm not feeling good. I need somebody to help me. I always felt like I could just do this all by myself, and that's not true, you know? And that's something I had to learn and train myself and my mind to ask for help.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear barrier signals (silence) and agency signals (realization of need to seek help) related to a cultural upbringing.

moment_text: So that's what I did. Um, so beyond just, I think. Traditional therapy, it was really building out this tool, you know, of practices, um, self-care practices, spiritual practices, um, just so I can show up in the world, you know, for myself and the people who were around me, you know, sort of take care of them as well. But first of all, I knew I had to take care of.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong agency signals (seeking support, seeking information) detailing a proactive approach to mental wellness.

moment_text: I think for the longest time I didn't sort of listen to what. Generally people would say, listen to your gut. You know, I didn't even know where in the world was my gut. Right. And I was like, what is, what is anybody talking? What is the gut? You know, knowing fully well that there was always a voice somewhere, you know, in some part of my body that was kind of leading me to one ask for help. To find that help to really, um, have the presence of mind, you know, to. Go do the research, you know, what has worked for people? You know, ask friends, you know, if they've gone through that, and in that moment start a conversation around mental health, that is just not, nothing really much to do with me, but also people around me, right? Because everybody's going through something and they're just waiting for somebody to ask them that question. You know, for them to kind of put it out and set it up on the table and then make it into an object of inquiry and say. I think that's troubling me as well. Why do you think that is? And I find that to be sort of the, the most human thing, you know, that we can do and also the most sort of woman thing that we can do.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong agency signals (sharing story, seeking support) and barrier signals (lack of language) within a peer community interaction.

moment_text: Um, I also, um, really gravitated towards poetry. Um, you know, the poetry of this Persian poet, lib, you know, was born in the same, um, tip city that I was born in, but two centuries apart. Um, you know, and his level of depth, you know, from where he spoke of feelings, um, and how intrepid he was, right, I think was very, very exciting to me. Um, the other, uh, person that I think completely shifted. My understanding of self, um, was, um, Isabel Eberhart, um, she was this really young, vibrant person who had. Coming through a life that at the very outset was a very dualistic life, right? Like she was born to, um, you know, parents who came from completely different, um, settings. You know, one was a princess and the other one you know, was a Greek Orthodox priest who had then become an anarchist, right? And she had been born. At the time when the French had control over Algeria. So she found herself, you know, really trying to find herself in the deserts of Algeria. And you know, she was in relationships, but she was never teed, right.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains agency signals (seeking information, seeking support) and a system interaction (media/literature) as a coping mechanism.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker is referring to the poet Ghalib, but the transcript says 'lib'. The reference to Isabel Eberhardt is also a form of finding strength and resilience in the stories of others.

moment_text: For her freedom was everything. Um, and the fact that she also dies in the desert from a flash flood, you know, that always sort of got to me and I was like. This is a life that is so untethered, um, so beyond meaning that I think it, it needs more exploration. And so I would teach that in my class. And I also had sort of created this, um, course around homesick, you know, traveling in search of home, um, and really sort of exploring the idea of what is home, especially for women, right? And what we think home is, is also part of the discovery of what it isn't. So, yeah. So that it's been a journey.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with agency signals (seeking_information) and barrier signals (education_gap) within a non-none system interaction (education_system).

moment_text: I think one of the reasons, um, I did sort of craft that course was because there were always students in my class who had come from somewhere they had left homes to come to this place and be in this place and often feeling completely sick. Literally physically sick, but also spiritually sick. Like they didn't know how to belong, right? Like the idea of home is always about belongingness and also about betrayal, right? Because we feel like home should be that place where we feel the safest. But for a lot of us, for me, it was not the safest of places. It was always a place that I was running from, you know?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with emotional signals (isolation) and barrier signals (lack_of_support) tied to a system interaction (family).

moment_text: As women, as a woman who grew up in India where, you know, your body is kind of not your own possession, right? It belongs to everyone and everyone outside of yourself. And everyone thinks they have claims, you know, over your body. You know, they think that somehow they can treat it, you know, dance with it, you know, hold it, possess it in a ways that, um, seemed really weird to me at that time. You know, even growing up. Um. So for me, I think really asking the question of what then does it mean to be alone? Right? What is aloneness for a woman who can never really be alone in the traditional sense, right?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with agency signals (asking_questions) and barrier signals (stigma) within a non-none system interaction (community).
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker is discussing the cultural context of bodily autonomy in India.

moment_text: I don't have children. I don't know if it was a conscious decision or an unconscious decision because I didn't want to bring kids into this world. Um. You know, and traumatized them in the way that, you know, I had been traumatized in the, in the space of the home, right? I didn't want to bring a child in who didn't feel safe in the home. And for some reason I thought that just because I had these patterns that I had unconsciously absorbed from my parents that I would transfer them to this child.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong emotional signals (fear) and agency signals (making_decisions) tied to a system interaction (family).
notes_for_reviewer: This moment touches on the concept of intergenerational trauma.

moment_text: I started to work with the women's NGOs in as soon as I graduated. Um. These were again, friends of mine, you know, who were sort of on their own mission to empower women, but they wanted to do that through education, you know, and a different kind of education, you know, that would address women's like, lived realities, you know, and help them find their home, you know, that they could stand alone and proud and still feel like, you know, they could contribute to their communities, right? Um, so that was one thing. And I think for me also. Trying to find home in the most unlikely of places, right? Like, I used to live with this group of friends, you know, when I graduated, um, a lot of my friends actually had been married off by that time, right? Um. I'd been sort of, um, asked to consider the idea of meeting somebody and getting married in that traditional sense.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with agency signals (seeking_support) and emotional signals (empowerment) within a peer community context.

moment_text: And, you know, all my life, I've not been ordinary, right? This is something I've always lived a very extraordinary life by design or by intention. Um, and I think that in some ways has served me well, but has also left me alone in many, many, many different ways, but something that I have. Owned it for myself. You know that here my power lies in being, um, alone. And in that aloneness I've been able to ground myself, you know, to tell myself the ways in which I can be safe here. That if there are no systems of power and control, you know, that I'm responding to, then I'm safe. Then I belong to myself. Right?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong agency signals (making_decisions, setting_boundaries) and a corresponding barrier signal (social_isolation).

moment_text: For me, it was always about, you know, my idea of home and what I experienced in a home were two very different things, you know, to sort of grow up in a home where you just felt unseen and not visible. You know, for me, visibility has to do with being loved, right? Visibility has to do with having the attention of something or someone. Visibility has to do with, um, somebody saying. I love you. I will take care of you and I will guide you. And I sort of didn't have that. And I think I've always searched that outside of myself in a person, an idea project, even a country, you know? That's why I came here to the United States in a way to be like, okay, can I find a home here? Like what would that look like?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with clear emotional signals (sadness, isolation) and barrier signals (lack_of_validation) tied to a system interaction (family).

moment_text: Very quickly realized that my home literally was in the spaces and the caverns of my mind and my heart, right? And for the longest time, I think my mind had denied my heart. Sort of the attention that it deserved. So my heart had in fact, sort of shive up, like I close my heart to a lot of things on the outside and very quickly realized that, you know, um, the heart has probably a hundred times the electromagnetic power, then the brain does. So if my heart is not open, then I'm not opening myself to experiences that can further elevate me, like, you know, on this spiritual journey. So, yeah.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong emotional signals (clarity, relief) and agency signals (making_decisions).

moment_text: So you're right, there's a difference between lonely and alone, and that's the difference I had to tease out for myself. From the longest time. I would just sit beside my bed and cry and say like, oh my gosh, like I am so lonely. There is no witness to my life right now. There's nobody to hug me or to take care of me or to do things around me that make me feel like I belong, you know?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong emotional signals (loneliness, sadness) and barrier signals (social_isolation).

moment_text: Um. But I think that was sort of the necessary first step, you know, to feel that alone, to feel lonely, for me to come back to examining what does aloneness mean for me and what has it done for me? Right? And I felt like what it has done for me is to literally just sit with myself, right? Often. You know, the fact that we kinda mirror ourselves in the eyes of others and therefore we see ourselves through their eyes, right? We don't carry our own mirrors, right? We are not looking at ourselves and saying, oh, this is what I look like on a daily basis. We don't do that. We look at other people in the way they're looking at us to understand us, and that's sort of the pattern, right? Whether it's in a job, whether it's in the eyes of a friend, whether it's a meetup, whether it's. Talking to an investor, whether it's talking to, you know, women who have been benefited by crea, you know, it's almost like they're the ones who are affirming and confirming to you who you think you should be. Right. The way you think about yourself, it has to be different than what other things about you, and that can only happen in the, you know, the context of aloneness, like sitting with yourself maybe out in the balcony, under the moonlight, with nothing except the sounds of the night. That's the moment you meet your subconscious. That's the moment you meet what I would call your soul, right? Um, and in that moment of meeting you realize that you are really not lonely, right? And that you had to be alone physically to be able to have that moment, and then everything turns right.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with strong emotional signals (loneliness, clarity) describing a significant internal realization about self-perception, which is a potential emotional pattern.

moment_text: For me, that was the turning point where I was just like, you know, I would just hug myself and say, wow, you did so great. You did so great. All this life, you felt like there was nobody for you, but yet you were protected, yet you were safe, right? You traveled to places that No girl, at least in, in my, you know, cultural context in my extended family has ever traveled. Like I've come across seven Cs, you know, to find these set of experiences. You know, I've taken the jump and I've done while being absolutely fearful. I haven't let fear sort of define what my next steps were. I have jumped, and I think I've learned from jumping too often that yes, you know, you can fall and you can hurt yourself, but you also ultimately have built that capacity. To take on pain in a way that a lot of people may not. And in that way, you have extended the boundaries of what you can do. So for me, I think that realization has been exactly how I've come home to myself, how I have come home to Shara, and Shara has become a complete person.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with strong emotional signals (empowerment, confidence) and agency signals (making_decisions) in the context of overcoming fear and cultural family norms.

moment_text: I feel first and foremost, um, I'm just a very empathetic person and I borrow that from my mother. She's a healer, she has been a doctor for the longest time, and I've seen how she's transformed like lives, you know, over the course of last 40 years that she's been practicing homeopathy, um, and how people always sort of come to her, maybe not even to take. I think she herself is a medicine to them because of the way in which they share and the way she listens. Um, and as a teacher, I think my. Um, if my standpoint is empathy, then I know that I'm going to position myself in a way where I will first and foremost listen to what a student has to say, right? And often students would come to me, and they're not just women students, they're also male students who would come to me and feel that they could be vulnerable in that moment because I will hold space for them. Right, and I will hold space in a way where they could share this and know that they could walk away with like maybe a moment of understanding themselves that they didn't have before and that they could then come back and maybe extend that understanding more. I think my course on Homesick and, you know, um, traveling Search of Home did exactly that. There were students who would come in and say, I am not feeling like I belong here. Like. You know, college was not feeling like home to them. And so I was like, okay, let's talk about that. So we would talk about their idea of home, and then I would share about the kind of disconnects I've had. So in that moment of disconnect, we would connect, right? That it doesn't have to be, our idea of home doesn't have to be a general idea of home and that we don't have to buy into it. That we can have very individual experiences of home and each one of them is valid. Right. It's all legitimate because it's me. I am feeling this, I am saying this. This is my truth, and if I can speak my truth, then I can live with my truth, right?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with clear barrier signals (social_isolation, lack_of_validation) and agency signals (speaking_up) within the education_system, highlighting a relational pattern.

moment_text: One of them just wanted to go travel the outs. I was like, I just, I just wanna go tracking and I wanna write about it. Can I do that? And I was like, do you think you can do that? The two things here, do you want to do that? And the other one is the ability. I think you have the ability to do it, but what you are saying is that there are too many things around my college going experience that I have to consider before I can allow that to happen. So I was like, let's look at some of those problems, right? Just like, I don't wanna disappoint my parents, you know, they're paying for my college education. It's an expensive school. What if I go, they, they're going to be disappointed in me. So I was like, let's set that aside. You know, think about what's gonna make you happy. Is it going to build capacity? Is it gonna build skills? Is it gonna build your writing skills? What do you think you can do with those writing skills as you go forward? And the technical pieces of that are easy to resolve, right? Where you could say, I can take a semester off. I can explore this idea, I can see where that takes me. Let me see what I feel about it, and you can come back to me and we can talk more, right? So it's really breaking down these pieces of the self that somehow keeps us away from what we want to do.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate with a clear barrier (fear_of_judgment from family) being addressed through agency (making_decisions, seeking_support), which constitutes a relational pattern.

moment_text: And I think I was very, very good at that. Um, and I say that with a lot of humility as well, because, um. This is a skill that doesn't have to do with my PhD. This is not to do with the fact that I'm highly trained and expert, but I've also worked around and listened to people, um, like really listened, you know, where they felt like they have been listened to, so that now they can do that work for themselves as well.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate by identifying a barrier signal (training_gap) in formal education (PhD) and contrasting it with the skill of listening, which is a potential systemic pattern.
notes_for_reviewer: The system_interaction is 'medical_training' as a proxy for high-level, formal education being contrasted with empathetic skills.

moment_text: So that's an excellent question. To be honest, I was actually thinking of it like yesterday. You know, I woke up with this feeling of like, good, I, you know. Stake, of course, like, you know, homesick traveling in search of home, but really pull that into building this community of people who are coming into Austin right now and are obviously feeling very homesick. Like I've very recently met people from like Norway and Sweden, you know, from Cameroon, and everybody seems to talk about how they miss home, yet they cannot return home. Um, and so that's the question that I've been asking myself. Could I create that safe place and build this sort of collective where people can be home and at the same time also start to redefine the very idea of home within themselves, right? And but do it in relation to each other. Right? And I think I'm positioned really well to kind of really.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 gate by identifying a clear barrier (social_isolation) for a community and proposing a solution, demonstrating agency and highlighting a potential protective factor.

moment_text: Great question. Um, two ways. One, of course is by reaching into the recesses of my own mind. Um, you know, I'm, I wanna march for myself, you know, I want to wake up doing that for myself. I want to have thoughts that make me feel like this is gonna be great, right? Um, but also at the same time to convey that, you know, to. My family members, right? Because I have two siblings, I have two brothers. You know, we've also lived in the same home, even though we may have had different perceptions and interpretations of it, but there is a profound disconnect there too. So I feel like I also need to keep sharing and keep that outflow of what I'm understanding about home, to kind of move into my traditional home as well, and to share that with my brothers, and also to share that with.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains agency signals (sharing_story) and barrier signals (social_isolation) within a family system interaction.

moment_text: My nieces and nephews, and I call them the new humans, you know, in my family because they are the new humans. Um, and I think I paid very specific attention to these new humans. Um. I have the same practice of really listening to them. Right? And there are moments where, you know, if my 6-year-old nephew is kind of at his dinner table and eating, I very consciously would make the decision to go and just sit by him. No words. No touching, no sharing of anything, just sitting by him because I wanted him to feel like just the presence of me is going to be okay, that he's safe, that we don't have to share anything in that moment. That 6-year-old doesn't need to feel the weightiness of an adult that are sitting at the table. They need to feel like. It's easy, it's fun. It's like I can say anything and it's going to be okay to feel safe and that you can do just by being there. So that's what I consciously do.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains strong agency signals (making_decisions to create safety) and addresses an implicit barrier (fear_of_judgment) within a family system interaction.

moment_text: And the other thing that I do for them is, um, two things. One is, um, the practice of havening or tapping. Where, you know, just about as they're about to go and sleep, you know, I would do that work for them, you know, which is just touch, that is calming. That makes them feel safer. That makes them feel like, oh, they can get into a dreamland and nothing is going to get them in that space. So I do it from, and then also. If there are certain things or ideas that have been trapped in their minds at that early and age from whatever they're looking at, right? Whether it could be the TV or something that an adult said or a teacher said, you know, to be able to bring that forth and say like, this is my biggest fear. So once they bring it forth, then they say, oh, actually, it is not that much of a fear. Right. And then when you're also doing the tapping and the ing, then they're able to feel calm in their bodies. You know, the body is not yet trying to keep so many scores that they won't be able to deal with it at some point of time.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains clear agency signals (speaking_up about fears) and emotional signals (fear, relief) within a family system interaction, demonstrating a method for overcoming barriers.

moment_text: Um, and then of course with, um, with just the women in my life, um, my friendships, um, you know, I call them like my soulmates, you know, women that I may not talk to for the next couple of months, but when we do, we go all out. It's like just being there for each other in those two hours that we are, you know, laid bare. You know, our lives are laid bare. We are laid bare, and we know that we are doing it for each other and doing it very safely so that each one of us can get up at the end of that conversation, carry on with our lives, but with a sense of empowerment again, and feeling that somebody has my back. That even though you're not literally there, like in words and spirit, you're there.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains strong emotional signals (empowerment, connection) and agency signals (sharing_story) within a peer community system interaction, highlighting a protective factor against barriers like social isolation.

moment_text: My whole life's work has been trying to ask the question like, what is a woman? Um, and therefore, I have only ever worked with girls and women, whether they were in some of the most economically depressed regions of India, or they were within the women's and gender studies programs. Again, very minority programs. Within sort of the US educational structure and then now with prea, which is about aging, you know, and aging and wisdom. Um, but you're also right. I think it was me trying to, um, by making women's lives visible and asking them the question of what is it that they want, right? Um. In a, in this lifetime that they've been brought into. And across the board, you know, across cultures, uncategorically, women have said respect and dignity. Right. They have never once said, I want more money, or, you know, I want more love. Or, you know, I want my children to do A, B, or C. It has always been respect and dignity, and to me that meant I need to be seen, I need to be listened to, and I need to be seen as somebody with full human capacity to be able to experience and understand and talk about the world.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment presents a systemic observation with clear barrier signals (lack_of_validation) and agency signals (speaking_up) that are explicitly cross-cultural.

moment_text: And so I started there, which was um, really building out curriculums for women so they can empower themselves. But also, and something you said already, which I hadn't thought of before, which is making them feel safe. Right When you feel empowered and you can stand up for yourself like a woman farmer, um, you know, whose lands had been dispossessed, you know, who had, you know, lost sort of her credibility in the village because, um, most of the time she would run away from a challenge and for her to stand up, like build a community. Find the right information that is gonna help us get her lands back and to stand up and say, this is mine. Nobody can take this from me. Or to see a hand pump mechanic, right? An untouchable woman coming from a tribal region to go 20 miles. You know, taking every single piece of a pipe that is required to set up or repair a hand pump, you know, which is how women and, you know, people in the villages draw water from. Um, and most of the time they have. Name waste pretty much because the government is pathetic. They do not come in and repair any of this stuff. So we had trained these women to go and repair these hand pumps, and they would go and do that facing such resistance initially, because obviously, you know. Water is a source of purity and pollution, right? And that's what the caste system is kind of built on. So an untouchable woman coming in to repair your hand pump is not going to do it for people who very clearly follow hierarchical lines, but they sort of power through that because in the absence of help, who else are they going to go to?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This is a powerful narrative of overcoming multiple, explicit systemic barriers (stigma, access_barrier) through strong acts of agency (self_advocacy, speaking_up).

moment_text: So really, um, again, you know, building this brand required talking to women, you know, who were aging, who were feeling completely unanchored, did not feel they were at home at all in their bodies. Because obviously when we are hitting menopause, our body is kind of revolting against us, right? It's like body itself is not a safe place to be in. You want to escape it. You are like, how do I escape this with, you know, medicine? How do I escape it by harming it? How do I escape it by completely ignoring it? Right? And my challenge here was how to make women feel that just by adding, you know, four. Organic ingredients to your scalp, you are going to soothe your parasympathetic nerve system.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains strong emotional signals (anxiety, fear) and barrier signals (stigma, lack_of_validation) directly tied to a specific life stage (menopause_transition) and system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The phrase 'how do I escape it by harming it' could be interpreted as a risk, so I've flagged it as mental_health_sensitivity.

moment_text: That's how you get to have an opening to come home, and that's how you get to sit in the home and say, this is gonna be okay. I'm going to be okay. Right. So that I think was my challenge and I think I have been very successful in meeting that challenge, in creating something which is that pure aligns with a woman's body makes them feel that they can be home And um, menopause is not a disease and we are not mad.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains a barrier signal ('stigma') and emotional signals ('confidence', 'empowerment') within a peer community system interaction.

moment_text: I love it. I just love this theme of coming home and I love the fact that you were able to, as you've been on your journey, uh, again, it's the through line, but applying it even to building out a brand and a product line, you know that it's for women, it's. Coming home for women. And so I really That's wonderful, cha. You should be really, you know, I really admire you. You should be really proud. I'm curious. I mean, I could talk to you forever. I'm curious, this is your platform today to give a message to women of all ages, of all backgrounds, of all cultures. What do you wanna share with them?
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains emotional signals ('validation', 'recognition') and a system interaction ('peer_community').
notes_for_reviewer: This moment is primarily the host validating the guest and setting up the next question.

moment_text: To not be afraid to be alone. You know, make that an intentional thing. Um. Even if you can be alone for 15 minutes, right? In a space where there is no distraction, there is nobody calling out your name. There's nobody asking for something where you are not asking anything of yourself either.
